Chedwell Overlook Road  ·  Chautauqua Lake

The Cottage on the Lake

A west-facing perch above Chautauqua—where summer evenings belong.

Summer at the cottage — hydrangeas in full bloom, chairs set for the lake.
The view west across Chautauqua — daylilies, dock, and open water.
Sunset — the nightly performance, never the same twice.
Another evening; another sky.
The dock — rods rigged, flag flying, the Fourth of July mood of any given Tuesday.
Across the water to the far shore — the quieter side of the lake.
A fair breeze and a bright afternoon — the reason you keep a boat.
The Miller Bell Tower at Chautauqua — the lake’s most famous landmark.

A Place on the Water

Chedwell Overlook

The cottage sits on Chedwell Overlook Road, on the high western bank of Chautauqua Lake, in that particular sliver of Western New York where the land leans gently down toward the water and the afternoon light lingers a little longer than it ought to. It is a modest house—grey shingle, white trim, a great arched window on the lake side that catches the evening sun like a lantern. There are hydrangeas at the foundation, four white Adirondack chairs on the lawn, and a dock at the foot of the hill with two rods and an American flag that I raise each morning out of a combination of habit and faith.

Chautauqua is not a flashy lake. It is seventeen miles of quiet water, vineyards on the ridges, small boats, grackles in the willows, and the occasional distant echo of a bell from the Institution across the way. It has been a place of rest and thought for nearly a hundred and fifty years, and whatever else one can say about the modern world, it has yet to find a way to ruin a Chautauqua sunset.

Every evening the sky puts on a performance for an audience of four lawn chairs, a flag, and whoever happens to have the good sense to be looking west.

We came to the cottage in the way most people come to lake houses: by accident, then by design. What was bought as a weekend retreat became the place the boys came home to in summer, the place the dogs learned to swim, the place a long marriage earned a second, slower rhythm after the office door closed on a Friday. Nancy planted the hydrangeas. I built the dock up, more or less, one weekend at a time. The Adirondack chairs we bought in a pack of four, and have never needed a fifth because whoever is over has the good grace to pull a chair from the deck.

The lake is the thing. In the morning it is glass, and a kayak leaves a single seam of wake that unzips the reflection of the far shore. By noon there are pontoons and small sailboats and the occasional waterskier working a patch of flat water down near the bell tower. In the late afternoon the wind tends to lay down and the light goes a kind of amber that photographers chase and never quite catch. At night, on the right evening, you can hear the chimes of the Miller Bell Tower across two miles of open water, so faint you almost think you imagined them.

The Bell Tower itself — visible from our dock on clear days — belongs to the Chautauqua Institution, the nineteenth-century camp for the religious and lifelong-learning movement that gave the lake its character. It is not our bell, strictly speaking, but it rings at five o’clock whether we have been working or fishing or napping in a chair, and it has the grace of all old institutions: you can ignore it, but you cannot quite stop listening for it.

This page is a small album of the cottage — mornings and evenings, the view west, a few sails, a few sunsets, the flag on the dock, the chairs on the lawn. The house itself may not stay in the family forever; life has a way of rearranging its rooms. But the evenings I have spent looking west across Chautauqua, with a drink in hand and no particular plan for the next hour, have been among the best hours of a long and lucky life. They are here, in these photographs, for as long as pixels last.
